Outlier Detection, Descriptive Statistics, Graphs & Association Tests¶

Input: Students_Performance_CLEANED.xlsx (output of the data-cleaning notebook)

Covers (per the planned workflow):

  1. Import clean dataset
  2. Outlier Detection (boxplots, before/after)
  3. Descriptive Statistics
  4. Frequency Tables
  5. Graphs (histograms, bar charts, scatter plots, Q-Q plots)
  6. Association Tests (Chi-square, Kruskal-Wallis)
  7. Export outlier-checked dataset

All plots are also saved as .jpg files in the working folder.

Step 1: Import Clean Dataset¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

FILE_PATH = r"C:\Users\kpriy\Downloads\Students_Performance_CLEANED.xlsx"
df = pd.read_excel(FILE_PATH)

# CGPA_Class should be treated as an ordered category throughout this notebook
class_order = ["General", "2nd Lower", "2nd Upper", "1st Class"]
df["CGPA_Class"] = pd.Categorical(df["CGPA_Class"], categories=class_order, ordered=True)

print("Shape:", df.shape)
df.head()

Shape: (1168, 31)
Out[1]:
Admission_Year Gender Age HSC_Year Semester Scholarship Uses_Transport Study_Hours Study_Sessions Learning_Mode ... Relationship_Status Co_Curricular Living_With Health_Issues Previous_SGPA Physical_Disability Current_CGPA Credits_Completed Family_Income CGPA_Class
0 2018 Male 24 2016 12 Yes No 3 2 Offline ... Single Yes Bachelor No 2.68 No 3.15 75 25000 2nd Lower
1 2021 Male 22 2020 4 Yes Yes 3 2 Online ... Single Yes Family No 2.68 No 3.15 36 100000 2nd Lower

3 2021 Male 20 2020 4 Yes No 1 3 Offline ... Single No Bachelor Yes 2.68 No 3.15 36 62488 2nd Lower
4 2021 Male 22 2019 4 Yes No 3 1 Online ... Relationship No Bachelor Yes 2.68 No 3.15 36 50000 2nd Lower

5 rows × 31 columns

Step 2: Outlier Detection¶

Approach used in this notebook (matching standard statistical practice): the 1.5×IQR rule is used only to flag candidate points for inspection where it is not used to automatically delete anything. For each flagged variable, we look at how the flagged values are distributed:

  • If many responses sit beyond the whisker (a genuine bulk region, e.g. a cluster of 30+ students with similar values), that's a real feature of the population, not an error, and is kept.
  • If a value is an isolated, single point, clearly separated from every other observation, and it is domain-implausible (not just statistically rare), it is treated as a likely data-entry error and removed.

The final keep/remove decision for each flagged variable is discussed after the "before" boxplots below.

In [2]:
numeric_cols = [
    "Age", "Admission_Year", "HSC_Year", "Semester", "Study_Hours", "Study_Sessions",
    "Social_Media_Hours", "Attendance", "Skill_Dev_Hours", "Previous_SGPA", "Current_CGPA",
    "Credits_Completed", "Family_Income",
]

def plot_boxplots(data, cols, title, filename):
    n = len(cols)
    n_cols = 4
    n_rows = int(np.ceil(n / n_cols))
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_rows, n_cols, figsize=(4 * n_cols, 4 * n_rows))
    axes = axes.flatten()
    for i, col in enumerate(cols):
        sns.boxplot(y=data[col], ax=axes[i], color="skyblue")
        axes[i].set_title(col, fontsize=10)
        axes[i].set_ylabel("")
    for j in range(len(cols), len(axes)):
        fig.delaxes(axes[j])
    fig.suptitle(title, fontsize=14)
    fig.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(filename, dpi=200, bbox_inches="tight")
    plt.show()

plot_boxplots(df, numeric_cols, "Boxplots BEFORE Outlier Review", "boxplots_before.jpg")
In [3]:
# Systematically flag candidate outliers with the 1.5xIQR rule, and show HOW they're distributed
# (a handful of values repeated many times = bulk region; a single unique value = isolated point)
for col in numeric_cols:
    q1, q3 = df[col].quantile(0.25), df[col].quantile(0.75)
    iqr = q3 - q1
    lo, hi = q1 - 1.5 * iqr, q3 + 1.5 * iqr
    flagged = df[(df[col] < lo) | (df[col] > hi)][col]
    print(f"--- {col} --- IQR whiskers: [{lo:.2f}, {hi:.2f}]  |  flagged points: {len(flagged)}")
    print(flagged.value_counts().sort_index())
    print()

Decisions, variable by variable:

Variable What was flagged Decision Reason
Age 26 (12 students), 27 (1) Keep Bulk cluster of older/mature students, plausible, not an error
Admission_Year 2013–2016 (13 students) Keep Bulk cluster, legitimate earlier admission cohort
HSC_Year 2012–2017 (114), 2022 (9) Keep Bulk clusters on both ends, legitimate cohort spread
Semester 21, 22, 24 (5 students) Keep Unusually high but not impossible for repeat/part-time students; flagged for your discretion
Study_Hours 8–13 hrs/day (22 students) Keep Bulk cluster of highly dedicated students, plausible
Study_Sessions 4–7 sessions/day (42 students) Keep Bulk cluster, plausible study pattern
Social_Media_Hours 8–16 hrs (70 students), 20 hrs (1 student) Remove only the single 20-hour row 8–16 is a real bulk cluster; 20 hours is an isolated, practically impossible value (leaves ~4 hrs/day for everything else)
Attendance 0, 2, 5, 10, ... up to 47 (36 students) Keep Low attendance is realistically possible; per your note, these are genuine, not errors
Skill_Dev_Hours 7–12 hrs (8 students) Keep Plausible for a small group of dedicated hobbyists
Previous_SGPA 0.00 (28 students), 0.13125 (1) Keep 0.00 is a legitimate bulk cluster (students without a prior SGPA yet); 0.13125 is unusual precision, possibly a typo, but retained since it's a plausible value
Current_CGPA 0.00 (28), 0.13125 (1), 0.5 (1), 1.42–1.75 (6) Keep Same reasoning as above, legitimate low-performing values, not impossible
Credits_Completed none flagged Keep No candidates
Family_Income 144,000–600,000 (58 students), 2,000,000 (1 student) Remove only the single 2,000,000 row 144,000–600,000 is a genuine, continuous spread of household incomes and is kept; 2,000,000 is an isolated point sitting more than 3x above the next-highest value (600,000) — it's plausible in isolation, but keeping it would let a single extreme case pull the model, so it's removed to keep the model more generalised

Net result: two rows are removed - the single Social_Media_Hours = 20 entry, and the single Family_Income = 2,000,000 entry.

In [4]:
# Remove the two confirmed outliers:
# 1) Social_Media_Hours = 20 (isolated, practically impossible value)
# 2) Family_Income = 2,000,000 (isolated extreme value, far beyond the next-highest income of
#    600,000; plausible on its own, but removed so the model generalises rather than being
#    pulled by a single extreme case)
before_n = len(df)
df = df[(df["Social_Media_Hours"] != 20) & (df["Family_Income"] != 2000000)].copy()
after_n = len(df)

print(f"Rows removed: {before_n - after_n}")
print(f"Shape before: {before_n} rows -> after: {after_n} rows")

Rows removed: 2
Shape before: 1168 rows -> after: 1166 rows
In [5]:
plot_boxplots(df, numeric_cols, "Boxplots AFTER Outlier Review", "boxplots_after.jpg")

Comparing the two figures: every variable looks essentially the same before and after, since only two rows were removed in total, given that almost all the flagged points were genuine bulk clusters, not errors. The visible (subtle) changes are in the Social_Media_Hours panel, where the single point at 20 is gone, and the Family_Income panel, where the single point at 2,000,000 is gone.

Additional Diagnostic Plots (Scatter Plots & Q-Q Plots)¶

Scatter plots help visually check the relationship between CGPA and other continuous variables, and Q-Q plots check how far each numeric variable departs from a normal distribution, which is relevant context for discussing distributional assumptions.

In [6]:
# Scatter plots: Current_CGPA against other continuous variables
scatter_pairs = ["Previous_SGPA", "Attendance", "Study_Hours", "Family_Income"]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))
axes = axes.flatten()
for i, col in enumerate(scatter_pairs):
    sns.regplot(x=df[col], y=df["Current_CGPA"], ax=axes[i],
                scatter_kws={"alpha": 0.4, "s": 15}, line_kws={"color": "red"})
    axes[i].set_title(f"Current_CGPA vs {col}")
fig.suptitle("Scatter Plots: Current CGPA vs Key Continuous Variables", fontsize=14)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("scatterplots_cgpa.jpg", dpi=200, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
In [7]:
# Q-Q plots: check normality of the two most relevant continuous variables
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 5))
stats.probplot(df["Current_CGPA"], dist="norm", plot=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title("Q-Q Plot: Current_CGPA")
stats.probplot(df["Family_Income"], dist="norm", plot=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title("Q-Q Plot: Family_Income")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("qqplots.jpg", dpi=200, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

Both Q-Q plots show clear departures from the straight reference line, especially Family_Income (heavily right-skewed, as expected for income data)

In [9]:
categorical_cols = [
    "Gender", "Scholarship", "Uses_Transport", "Learning_Mode", "Uses_Smartphone", "Has_Computer",
    "English_Level", "Probation", "Suspension", "Teacher_Consultation", "Skills", "Interest_Area",
    "Relationship_Status", "Co_Curricular", "Living_With", "Health_Issues", "Physical_Disability",
]

# Categorical variables: mode (most common category) and how dominant it is
summary_rows = []
for col in categorical_cols:
    counts = df[col].value_counts()
    summary_rows.append({
        "Variable": col,
        "Mode (most common)": counts.index[0],
        "Count": counts.iloc[0],
        "% of sample": round(100 * counts.iloc[0] / len(df), 1),
    })
pd.DataFrame(summary_rows)

Step 5: Graphs¶

A few variables (Age, Admission_Year, HSC_Year, Study_Hours, Study_Sessions, Social_Media_Hours, Skill_Dev_Hours) only take whole-number values spread across a small range. With histogram’s default automatic bin width, bins can land between two integers and pick up no observations, showing as a gap between bars even though the variable itself is fine. These columns are plotted with one bin per integer value below so the bars sit flush against each other; the remaining, genuinely continuous variables keep the default automatic binning.

In [11]:
# Histograms for all numeric variables
# Discrete count-type variables get one bin per integer value (discrete=True) so bars are
# contiguous with no gaps; the remaining continuous variables use automatic binning as before.
discrete_cols = {
    "Age", "Admission_Year", "HSC_Year", "Study_Hours", "Study_Sessions",
    "Social_Media_Hours", "Skill_Dev_Hours",
}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 4, figsize=(18, 16))
axes = axes.flatten()
for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
    sns.histplot(df[col], ax=axes[i], kde=True, color="cornflowerblue", discrete=(col in discrete_cols))
    axes[i].set_title(col, fontsize=10)
for j in range(len(numeric_cols), len(axes)):
    fig.delaxes(axes[j])
fig.suptitle("Histograms of Numeric Variables", fontsize=14)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("histograms_numeric.jpg", dpi=200, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
In [12]:
# Bar charts for all categorical variables (incl. the outcome variable)
bar_cols = categorical_cols + ["CGPA_Class"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(5, 4, figsize=(20, 20))
axes = axes.flatten()
for i, col in enumerate(bar_cols):
    order = df[col].value_counts().index
    sns.countplot(y=df[col], order=order, ax=axes[i], color="mediumseagreen")
    axes[i].set_title(col, fontsize=10)
    axes[i].set_ylabel("")
for j in range(len(bar_cols), len(axes)):
    fig.delaxes(axes[j])
fig.suptitle("Bar Charts of Categorical Variables", fontsize=14)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("barcharts_categorical.jpg", dpi=200, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

Step 6: Association Tests¶

  • Categorical predictors vs. CGPA_Class: Chi-square test of independence.
  • Continuous predictors vs. CGPA_Class: Kruskal-Wallis test (a non-parametric alternative to ANOVA seems appropriate here since CGPA_Class is ordinal and several continuous variables are skewed, as seen in the Q-Q plots above).

Caveat: the chi-square test assumes expected cell counts of at least 5 in each cell. A few categorical variables here (e.g. Skills, Interest_Area) have some sparse categories, so those specific results should be interpreted with some caution even though the test still runs.

In [13]:
# Chi-square tests: each categorical variable vs CGPA_Class
chi2_results = []
for col in categorical_cols:
    contingency = pd.crosstab(df[col], df["CGPA_Class"])
    chi2, p, dof, expected = stats.chi2_contingency(contingency)
    chi2_results.append({"Variable": col, "Chi2": round(chi2, 3), "df": dof, "p-value": round(p, 4)})

chi2_df = pd.DataFrame(chi2_results).sort_values("p-value")
chi2_df["Significant (p<0.05)"] = chi2_df["p-value"] < 0.05
chi2_df
Out[13]:
Variable Chi2 df p-value Significant (p<0.05)
1 Scholarship 65.152 3 0.0000 True
7 Probation 113.024 3 0.0000 True
6 English_Level 38.265 6 0.0000 True
11 Interest_Area 74.021 27 0.0000 True
10 Skills 57.224 21 0.0000 True
12 Relationship_Status 28.780 3 0.0000 True
8 Suspension 13.116 3 0.0044 True
9 Teacher_Consultation 10.929 3 0.0121 True
5 Has_Computer 9.228 3 0.0264 True
3 Learning_Mode 7.656 3 0.0537 False
13 Co_Curricular 7.386 3 0.0606 False
15 Health_Issues 7.102 3 0.0687 False
16 Physical_Disability 6.762 3 0.0799 False
14 Living_With 6.188 3 0.1028 False
0 Gender 5.188 3 0.1585 False
2 Uses_Transport 3.212 3 0.3601 False
4 Uses_Smartphone 1.143 3 0.7667 False
In [14]:
# Kruskal-Wallis tests: each continuous variable vs CGPA_Class
# (Current_CGPA is excluded -- CGPA_Class is derived directly from it, so testing it against itself
#  would be circular)
continuous_predictors = [c for c in numeric_cols if c != "Current_CGPA"]

kw_results = []
for col in continuous_predictors:
    groups = [df.loc[df["CGPA_Class"] == cls, col].dropna() for cls in class_order]
    stat_val, p = stats.kruskal(*groups)
    kw_results.append({"Variable": col, "H-statistic": round(stat_val, 3), "p-value": round(p, 4)})

kw_df = pd.DataFrame(kw_results).sort_values("p-value")
kw_df["Significant (p<0.05)"] = kw_df["p-value"] < 0.05
kw_df
Out[14]:
Variable H-statistic p-value Significant (p<0.05)
7 Attendance 63.117 0.0000 True
9 Previous_SGPA 470.597 0.0000 True
11 Family_Income 42.061 0.0000 True
2 HSC_Year 22.335 0.0001 True
6 Social_Media_Hours 18.609 0.0003 True
10 Credits_Completed 15.057 0.0018 True
1 Admission_Year 9.381 0.0246 True
0 Age 8.472 0.0372 True
3 Semester 6.535 0.0883 False
5 Study_Sessions 5.744 0.1248 False
8 Skill_Dev_Hours 4.813 0.1860 False
4 Study_Hours 2.221 0.5279 False

Variables with Significant = True show a statistically detectable association with CGPA class in this sample, and are good candidates to prioritize when you get to forward/backward selection for the ordinal logistic regression. A non-significant result here doesn't necessarily mean a variable is useless in the full model (interactions and confounding can change things), but it's a useful first-pass screen.

Step 7: Export Outlier-Reviewed Dataset¶

In [15]:
OUTPUT_PATH_XLSX = "Students_Performance_OUTLIER_CHECKED.xlsx"
OUTPUT_PATH_CSV = "Students_Performance_OUTLIER_CHECKED.csv"

df.to_excel(OUTPUT_PATH_XLSX, index=False)
df.to_csv(OUTPUT_PATH_CSV, index=False)

print("Final shape:", df.shape)
print(f"Saved to:\n  {OUTPUT_PATH_XLSX}\n  {OUTPUT_PATH_CSV}")

Final shape: (1166, 31)
Saved to:
  Students_Performance_OUTLIER_CHECKED.xlsx
  Students_Performance_OUTLIER_CHECKED.csv

Summary¶

Step Result
Outlier review 2 rows removed (Social_Media_Hours = 20, and Family_Income = 2,000,000 — both isolated, extreme single points); all other flagged values were genuine bulk clusters and were retained
Descriptive statistics Computed for all numeric and categorical variables
Frequency tables Computed for all categorical variables and CGPA_Class
Graphs Histograms, bar charts, scatter plots, and Q-Q plots generated and saved as .jpg
Association tests Chi-square (categorical) and Kruskal-Wallis (continuous) run against CGPA_Class
Final dataset Exported as .xlsx and .csv, ready for the ordinal logistic regression stage

Saved image files (in the working folder): boxplots_before.jpg, boxplots_after.jpg, scatterplots_cgpa.jpg, qqplots.jpg, histograms_numeric.jpg, barcharts_categorical.jpg
